original_abstract: Since coming to power in 2010, the Hungarian government, led by the populist radical right (PRR) Fidesz party, has gained international notoriety for reshaping domestic institutions to fit an illiberal political vision. This systemic democratic backsliding has triggered a protracted conflict with the European Union, reaching a flashpoint in April 2022 when the Commission invoked its new rule of law conditionality mechanism. Utilizing a model of cross-sectoral bargaining, this article examines how the standoff over democratic standards has bled into the realm of international security. We argue that Hungary has increasingly utilized "hostage-taking" strategies, linking domestic disputes to critical foreign policy decisions—most notably the EU’s multi-billion euro aid packages for Ukraine and NATO enlargement. While the Common Foreign and Security Policy (CFSP) was traditionally viewed as a professionalized, insulated domain of consensus, our findings reveal a deliberate instrumentalization of foreign policy to extract concessions in intra-EU politics. This shift reflects a broader trend toward the populist "domestication" of foreign policy, where international stability is traded for domestic political leverage.
humanized_abstract: Since 2010, the new right-wing populist party, Fidesz, elected by the majority of the population in Hungary, has steadily implemented reforms towards transforming the system into an illiberal and democratic state while conforming to a lower standard of the rule of law. The policies implemented by Prime Minister Orbán caused an international political turmoil, and particularly a long term conflict with the European Union. The conflict has increased significantly in April 2022, when the European Commission decided to impose penalties in line with the conditionality mechanism regarding rule of law introduced by the von der Leyen Commission. Our research uses a framework of cross-sectoral bargaining to illustrate the way in which the democracy vs autocracy conflict spilled over to the field of security. We describe and explain in detail the manner in which this conflict influenced relations to the national security of the EU members states. Specifically, we have identified the tactic of hostage-taking deployed by Hungary which turns a domestic controversy into a crucial foreign policy issue, such as the allocation of large sums of EU assistance to Ukraine and NATO expansion. While the European Common Foreign and Security Policy has generally been seen as being characterised by a professional bureaucratic elite consensus to ensure stability of the international system and has been protected from domestic political turmoil, our analysis shows that, in this instance, foreign affairs has been deliberately used in order to extract concessions in domestic EU politics, which is one of the expressions of the populist process known as the “domestication of foreign affairs”.
